_readme: Times are Unix epoch SECONDS in UTC. Render in America/New_York (see `timezone`) with Intl; in JS, new Date(seconds * 1000).
Optional fields are ALWAYS present with an explicit null when absent — never key-omitted, never 0 or "". Required booleans are true/false.

This file is already post-moderation: hidden events excluded, display_name overrides applied. It carries the same events the ICS feeds publish (same moderation + retention rules), reshaped for a website.
Occurrences nest under events (the site's unit is one card per event) — deliberately not the flat per-VEVENT iCal shape.

venues.name: King Plow Arts Center
venues.street: 887 W Marietta St NW, Suite J-107

venues.name: Conyers Rockdale Council for the Arts

events.name: Basura
events.description_short: World-premiere musical from Gloria and Emily Estefan: Paraguayan kids build an orchestra out of landfill scrap.
events.description_full: A world-premiere musical drawn from the real Recycled Orchestra of Paraguay, in which a music teacher starts an ensemble for students who have no instruments, so they make their own — paint cans and a fork become a violin, an oil drum a cello. Gloria Estefan and her daughter Emily Estefan wrote the songs; the book is by Karen Zacarías, with Michael Greif (Rent, Dear Evan Hansen) directing and Alex Lacamoire (Hamilton) handling orchestrations. Based on the documentary Landfill Harmonic. Runs through July 12.

events.name: It Had to Be You
events.description_short: Screwball comedy: a fading actress takes a TV commercial director hostage on a snowbound Christmas Eve.
events.description_full: In Renée Taylor and Joseph Bologna's screwball comedy, a fading actress snowed in on Christmas Eve takes a TV commercial director hostage, angling to turn the standoff into both a romance and her big break. Taylor and Bologna, the real-life married team behind Lovers and Other Strangers, originally wrote and starred in it themselves. Scott Ross directs for Merely Players. Runs through July 26.

events.name: Spinoff! The One Man Show(girl)
events.description_short: One man sings Broadway's leading ladies — a 30-seat lounge cabaret, one drink ticket included, 21 and up.
events.description_full: Claudio Pestana, a regular on Atlanta's musical-theatre stages, works through the numbers Broadway's most celebrated divas made famous, from classic songbook to modern showstoppers. His local credits include Jersey Boys with City Springs Theatre Company and Assassins at the Jennie T. Anderson Theatre. Presented by The Strand Theatre in a 30-seat lounge setting, with one drink ticket per ticket and the bar open after the show; guests must be 21 or older.

events.name: Baby Bipolar
events.description_short: Atlanta comedian Maria Mainelli's solo hour about getting a bipolar 1 diagnosis and deciding it doesn't run the story.
events.description_full: Maria Mainelli has feelings about MyChart and at least one bad therapist review in her drafts. Her solo show follows one woman from a bipolar 1 diagnosis to a life where that diagnosis doesn't have to be the bad part. Mainelli, an Atlanta-based writer and comedian who has performed across the US and UK, arrives fresh off touring her debut hour, Fly Trap, and is a vocal mental-health advocate. Presented by 7 Stages. Advisory: strong language and themes some may find triggering.

events.name: Atlanta Experimental Fest
events.description_short: A festival showcase of experimental film: abstract animation, personal documentaries, surreal narratives, and handmade cinema.
events.description_full: A festival screening devoted to independent filmmakers working outside conventional storytelling. The lineup ranges across abstract animation, personal documentaries, surreal narratives, handmade cinema, and work that resists genre labels altogether. Presented by 7 Stages, the Little Five Points company with a long history of experimental programming.

events.name: Jukebox Giants: Disco Fever!
events.description_short: A song-and-dance revue of disco hits — Donna Summer, Bee Gees, and ABBA covers with a live band.
events.description_full: A revue of 1970s disco hits, with singers and dancers backed by a live band working through songs made famous by Donna Summer, the Bee Gees, ABBA, and others. Expect sequins, choreography, and a crowd on its feet by the second number. Presented by the Strand Theatre. Note: the production may include flashing lights.

events.name: The Wizard of Oz
events.description_short: The 1939 MGM songbook onstage, Toto played by a puppet, from Walton County's community theatre.
events.description_full: The stage adaptation of the 1939 MGM film, with the Harold Arlen and E. Y. Harburg songs intact, 'Over the Rainbow' included. A Kansas farm girl is swept into Oz and heads down the yellow brick road with a scarecrow, a tin man, and a cowardly lion, hoping the Wizard can send her home. In this production Toto is a puppet in the Lion King mold, and the Munchkins are played mostly by children. Marc Hammes directs for On Stage Playhouse, Walton County's community theatre; runs through July 26.

events.name: Atlanta Micro Short Film Festival
events.description_short: Over 120 micro shorts in one afternoon, each six minutes or less, from animation to horror to documentary.
events.description_full: A single fast-moving afternoon packed with more than 120 short films, every one of them clocking in at six minutes or under. Filmmakers from around the world contribute across the board: animation, horror, documentary, comedy, drama, and experimental work. Presented by 7 Stages, in a theatre that started life as a movie house.

events.name: A Midsummer Night's Dream
events.description_short: Shakespeare's forest comedy of runaway lovers, amateur actors, and fairies with a misapplied love potion.
events.description_full: Shakespeare's comedy of one moonlit night in the woods outside Athens. Four runaway lovers, a band of amateur actors, and a feuding fairy king and queen cross paths, and a mischievous sprite's love potion lands on the wrong eyelids. Before morning, affections have swapped and the weaver Bottom is wearing a donkey's head. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed with food and drink served at the table in its Elizabethan-style playhouse.

events.name: Launchpad 2.0 - BlerdMan
events.description_short: An early look at a new play in development, part of Theatrical Outfit's Launchpad 2.0 new-works festival.
events.description_full: A new play getting an early public showing as part of Launchpad 2.0, Theatrical Outfit's development series for work still being built. It is one of four new plays performing in rotation across the festival, alongside The Penelopes, Queen (of the American Nail Salon), and I'm a Mother Fucking Superstar. Performances of BlerdMan run through July 25.

events.name: Launchpad 2.0 - The Penelopes
events.description_short: An early staging of a new play in development, from Theatrical Outfit's Launchpad 2.0 new-works series.
events.description_full: An early look at a new play still taking shape, presented as part of Launchpad 2.0, Theatrical Outfit's development series for new work. It shares the slate with three other new plays running in rotation. Runs through July 25.

events.name: The Foreigner
events.description_short: Larry Shue's 1984 farce: a painfully shy Englishman fakes speaking no English, and a Georgia fishing lodge spills its secrets.
events.description_full: Larry Shue's much-revived 1984 comedy, winner of two Obie Awards. Charlie Baker, shy to the point of paralysis, arrives at a rural Georgia fishing lodge, where a well-meaning friend passes him off as a foreigner who understands no English. The other guests promptly air their secrets, schemes, and romances right in front of him, and Charlie finds unexpected nerve in playing along. A community production from Renegade Theatre Company, which donates a portion of each show's proceeds to charity.

events.name: The Somewhat True Tale of Robin Hood
events.description_short: Mary Lynn Dobson's Monty Python-flavored Robin Hood spoof, with spoon-wielding Merry Men and dessert at intermission.
events.description_full: Mary Lynn Dobson's parody sends up the Robin Hood legend in full Monty Python spirit: a preening hero in green, a damsel fixated on her skin conditions, a sheriff who would rather be bowling, and a band of Merry Men armed with spoons and a collective IQ of six. A cheerful "Town's Guy" keeps wandering into scenes he has no business in. Walker Scott directs for Lionheart Theatre, the Norcross community company, and every ticket includes dessert at intermission. Runs through July 19.

events.name: Launchpad 2.0 - Queen (of the American Nail Salon)
events.description_short: One of four new plays in rotation for Launchpad 2.0, Theatrical Outfit's new-works development series.
events.description_full: A new play presented through Launchpad 2.0, Theatrical Outfit's development series for new work. Four new plays share the run in rotation, each an early look at a script still taking shape, with the audience among its first witnesses. In rotation through July 26.

events.name: Wit & Wisdom
events.description_short: Three Smoke on the Mountain castmates sing Broadway's roles for grown-ups — 30 seats, 21 and up.
events.description_full: Jeff Cooper, Blaine Clotfelter, and Vickie Zuffoletti, last together in the Strand's 2024 Smoke on the Mountain, reunite for a set of songs written for the Broadway canon's decidedly non-ingenue characters — the wisdom and humor that come with age, sung by people who have it. Zuffoletti curates and hosts the Lumiere Lounge Cabaret Series, of which this is part. Seating is capped at 30, each ticket includes a drink ticket, and the room is 21 and up; the lounge stays open after the show.

events.name: Brave Little Tailor
events.description_short: The Grimms' fairy tale of a boastful tailor, seven swatted flies, and some very gullible giants, from Pumphouse Players.
events.description_full: A stage adaptation of the Brothers Grimm fairy tale. A tailor kills seven flies with one swat, stitches himself a belt boasting "Seven at One Blow," and lets everyone assume he means seven men. On the strength of that misunderstanding he takes on giants and a scheming king, armed with nothing but nerve and quick thinking. From Pumphouse Players, the Cartersville community theatre, which last staged the tale in 2019.

events.name: Launchpad 2.0 - I'm a Mother Fucking Superstar
events.description_short: A new play in development, one of four rotating in Theatrical Outfit's Launchpad 2.0 new-works series.
events.description_full: A developmental staging of a new play from Theatrical Outfit's Launchpad 2.0 series — a script still being shaped, put on its feet in front of an audience while the work is in progress. It rotates in repertory with three other new plays, with the cycle running through July 26.

events.name: POTUS
events.description_short: Selina Fillinger's Broadway farce: seven women scramble to keep a catastrophically inept president out of trouble.
events.description_full: Selina Fillinger's 2022 Broadway farce about the seven women who actually keep the White House running. When the president's stray four-letter word snowballs from PR headache into global crisis, the beleaguered women in his orbit scramble to keep him upright and the country intact. The Washington Post's critic likened it to a cross between SNL and Veep. From Live Arts Theatre; recommended for ages 18 and up for severe language, adult situations, and violence.

events.name: Bourbon Bash
events.description_short: Whiskey tasting festival: 60-plus bourbons and whiskies from more than 20 distilleries, with a people's-choice vote.
events.description_full: A walk-around whiskey tasting from the Fox Theatre: more than 60 bourbons and whiskies poured by upwards of 20 distilleries, with bourbon-inspired bites alongside. Every ticket covers unlimited tastings and the food, and attendees cast ballots for the standout whiskey of the day. Admission includes a Lexus-branded gift; drinks beyond the tasting pours cost extra.

events.name: Monty Python's Spamalot
events.description_short: Eric Idle's Tony-winning Arthurian spoof — killer rabbits, taunting Frenchmen, coconut-shell hoofbeats — in a national touring production.
events.description_full: King Arthur recruits his knights and quests for the Grail past killer rabbits, taunting French soldiers, the Knights Who Say Ni, and a Lady of the Lake who resents her lack of stage time. Eric Idle adapted the Pythons' 1975 Holy Grail film into a musical, writing the songs with John Du Prez, including "Always Look on the Bright Side of Life." The original 2005 Broadway production won three Tony Awards, Best Musical among them. This is a national touring production.

events.name: The Great American Trailer Park Musical
events.description_short: Nehls and Kelso's trashy, big-hearted comedy of adultery, agoraphobia, and hurricanes at a Florida trailer park.
events.description_full: David Nehls and Betsy Kelso's musical comedy, an Off-Broadway hit in 2005, set at Armadillo Acres trailer park in Starke, Florida. Jeannie hasn't stepped outside her trailer in twenty years; her tollbooth-collector husband Norbert is losing patience; and Pippi, a stripper hiding out from a marker-huffing ex with a gun named Bertha, has just moved in next door. The affair that follows kicks up a storm in more ways than one. From New Depot Players, the Conyers community theatre.

events.name: Little Shop
events.description_short: Menken and Ashman's doo-wop horror comedy about a skid-row florist and the plant that drinks blood.
events.description_full: Alan Menken and Howard Ashman's 1982 horror-comedy musical. Seymour Krelborn, a meek clerk in a failing skid-row flower shop, finds a strange plant after a solar eclipse and names it Audrey II, after the co-worker he loves. The plant turns out to run on human blood, and as it grows, Seymour's fortunes and its appetite grow together. The score works in early-sixties rock and roll, doo-wop, and Motown. Jared Wright and Katherine Coyne direct for Southside Theatre Guild, Metro Atlanta's oldest all-volunteer community theatre. Runs through August 2.

events.name: Cinderella - Prince Street Players Version
events.description_short: The Prince Street Players musical of Perrault's Cinderella, staged by Winder-Barrow's Children's PLAYhouse with an all-ages cast.
events.description_full: A musical retelling of Charles Perrault's fairy tale, the one with the cinders, the slipper, and the midnight deadline, in the Prince Street Players version: adaptation and lyrics by Jim Eiler, music by Eiler and Jeanne Bargy. Billie Nye-Muller directs a community cast of children and adults for Winder-Barrow Community Theatre's Children's PLAYhouse. Proceeds from the show support Feeding Barrow, a weekend meal program for Barrow County students. Runs through July 26.

events.name: Luke Null
events.description_short: Musical comedy from a former SNL featured player — dirty songs, catchy hooks, 90 minutes, no intermission.
events.description_full: Luke Null does stand-up with a guitar: filthy lyrics set to melodies that stick, a style he built in Chicago clubs before a season as a featured player on Saturday Night Live. His 2024 special, "Pretty Songs, Dirty Words," now free on YouTube, tells you exactly what you're in for. Part of the Comedy at The Strand series. 90 minutes, no intermission.

events.name: Launchpad 2.0 Residency Workshop - Triune
events.description_short: A workshop showing of Triune, a new play in development, from Theatrical Outfit's Launchpad 2.0 residency.
events.description_full: A workshop presentation of Triune, a new play still taking shape — put on its feet in front of an audience while the writing is in progress. Part of Launchpad 2.0, Theatrical Outfit's residency program for developing new work.

events.name: Launchpad 2.0 Residency Workshop - In Rapping Shakespeare
events.description_short: One-night workshop showing of a new work in development, from Theatrical Outfit's Launchpad 2.0 residency.
events.description_full: A workshop presentation of a new work still taking shape, with the staging in progress and the audience in on the process. One of the residency showings closing out Launchpad 2.0, Theatrical Outfit's new-play development program.

events.name: Grease Sing-A-Long (1978)
events.description_short: The 1978 movie musical with on-screen lyrics — sing every word, rated PG, 105 minutes.
events.description_full: The 1978 film with John Travolta and Olivia Newton-John as Danny and Sandy, screened sing-along style with on-screen lyrics for "Summer Nights," "Greased Lightnin'," and the rest. A summer romance collides with 1950s high-school social order when the pair land at the same school. Rated PG, 1 hour 45 minutes, and a live pre-show concert on the house Mighty Allen Theatre Organ begins thirty minutes before the film.

events.name: Play Haven Development Series: Hannah & Greta, or the Cost of living the Dream
events.description_short: A new play by Bobbi Twiggs in development, the first presentation of Play Haven's series.
events.description_full: An early look at a new play by Bobbi Twiggs, still taking shape and shown here as the first presentation of Play Haven's development series. Staged in the rehearsal hall rather than on the mainstage, with the audience among the work's first witnesses. Hosted by Theatrical Outfit.

events.name: Choir Boy
events.description_short: Tarell Alvin McCraney's coming-of-age play about a gifted queer student leading his prep school's gospel choir.
events.description_full: Tarell Alvin McCraney's coming-of-age drama, threaded throughout with gospel spirituals. Pharus, the most gifted singer at an elite prep school devoted to raising young Black men into leaders, wins the coveted post of choir lead, and finds that being both talented and unmistakably himself puts him at odds with classmates and tradition alike. McCraney wrote the Oscar-winning film Moonlight, and this play reached Broadway in 2019 with a Tony nomination for Best Play. Presented by Actor's Express.

events.name: The Comedy of Errors
events.description_short: Shakespeare's shortest, most slapstick comedy: two sets of twins, one city, and mistaken identity run gloriously amok.
events.description_full: Shakespeare's shortest and most farcical play: two sets of identical twins, separated at birth as infants, end up in the same city without knowing it, and the mistaken identities pile up into wrongful beatings, an arrest, and accusations of madness and demonic possession. Adam King directs for the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed with the playhouse's table seating and food and drink service. Part of a three-comedy repertory alongside The Taming of the Shrew and Twelfth Night; performances run through September 5.

events.name: The Taming of the Shrew
events.description_short: Shakespeare's battle-of-the-sexes comedy with an all-female+ cast, one of three comedies in an August repertory.
events.description_full: Headstrong Katherine cannot marry off fast enough to suit her father, and her younger sister cannot marry until she does. Enter Petruchio, a bachelor as loud as Katherine is sharp, and the sparring begins. Whether the play's "taming" is comedy or cruelty has been argued for centuries; here an all-female+ cast, directed by Rachel Frawley, takes it on for the Atlanta Shakespeare Company. Part of an August repertory alternating three Shakespeare comedies; this production runs through September 3.

events.name: H.M.S. Pinafore
events.description_short: Gilbert and Sullivan's 1878 comic opera: a captain's daughter, a common sailor, and a shipload of class satire.
events.description_full: Gilbert and Sullivan's first international hit, from 1878. Aboard a Royal Navy warship, the captain's daughter Josephine loves Ralph Rackstraw, a lowly sailor, while her father has promised her to Sir Joseph Porter, a First Lord of the Admiralty who rose to run the Navy without ever going to sea. Cue elopement plans, a chorus of sisters, cousins, and aunts, and a late revelation that turns the whole ship upside down. From the Front Porch Players, a Clayton County community theatre; runs through August 16.

events.name: Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street (2026)
events.description_short: Sondheim's 1979 revenge musical: a wronged barber, a corrupt judge, and Mrs. Lovett's suspiciously well-stocked pie shop.
events.description_full: Stephen Sondheim and Hugh Wheeler's 1979 musical thriller, based on Christopher Bond's play. Benjamin Barker returns from wrongful exile as Sweeney Todd, reopens his barbershop above Mrs. Lovett's failing pie shop, and sharpens his razors for the judge who destroyed his family. His customers, meanwhile, start finding their way into the pies. Charlie Miller directs for Onstage Atlanta; runs through August 30.

events.name: Twelfth Night
events.description_short: Shakespeare's shipwreck comedy of twins, disguise, and yellow stockings, in the Atlanta Shakespeare Company's three-comedy August repertory.
events.description_full: Shipwrecked on the coast of Illyria and believing her twin brother drowned, Viola disguises herself as a young man and enters Duke Orsino's service, promptly tangling herself into a love triangle. Meanwhile, a forged letter convinces the pompous steward Malvolio that yellow stockings are the way to his lady's heart. Mary Ruth Ralston directs for the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, with dinner-table seating and drink service during the show. Part of the August Threepeat, a repertory rotating three Shakespeare comedies nightly; this one plays through September 4.

events.name: Staged Readings
events.description_short: Actors read never-before-produced scripts by local playwrights — book in hand, staging kept minimal.
events.description_full: New scripts by local playwrights, none of them ever produced, read aloud by actors with book in hand and little more than a sketch of staging. The words carry the whole show, and the audience gets first crack at them. Presented by Merely Players.

events.name: Jazz Talks with Trey Wright
events.description_short: Coltrane's A Love Supreme, unpacked by jazz guitarist and KSU professor Trey Wright; 30 seats, 21+, drink included.
events.description_full: An interactive talk on John Coltrane and A Love Supreme, his 1965 landmark, led by Trey Wright, who teaches jazz guitar and jazz history at Kennesaw State. Wright is a working guitarist himself: he has played the Montreux Jazz Festival, and his trio's album Thinking Out Loud reached the first round of Grammy consideration in the jazz instrumental category. Part of the Jazz At The Strand series. Admission includes one drink ticket; seating is capped at 30, and guests must be 21 or older.

events.name: All Shook Up
events.description_short: The Elvis jukebox musical: a leather-clad roustabout shakes up a buttoned-down 1955 town, Twelfth Night style.
events.description_full: A jukebox musical built on the Elvis Presley songbook, with a book by Joe DiPietro (Memphis) that borrows its plot from Twelfth Night. In the summer of 1955, a guitar-playing roustabout roars into a town so square its mayor enforces a Decency Act against loud music, public necking, and tight pants. A lovestruck young mechanic named Natalie glues on a beard to get closer to him, and the romantic entanglements multiply from there, set to "Jailhouse Rock," "Hound Dog," and "Can't Help Falling in Love." From Marietta Theatre Company; the show premiered on Broadway in 2005.

events.name: Sidekicks Show
events.description_short: Improv comedy from the Southside Sidekicks, Southside Theatre Guild's in-house troupe.
events.description_full: A night of improv comedy from the Southside Sidekicks, the in-house troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973. Scenes built on the spot, shaped by whatever the audience throws at them — no two shows the same.

events.name: Dad's Garage 2026
events.description_short: Improvisers from Atlanta's Dad's Garage make the whole show up from audience suggestions.
events.description_full: Performers from Dad's Garage, Atlanta's long-running improv comedy company, build the entire show in the moment: scenes, characters, and games sparked by whatever the audience calls out. No script, no repeats. Presented by Stage Door Theatre.

events.name: Silent Film at The Strand: Sunrise (1927)
events.description_short: F.W. Murnau's 1927 silent masterpiece, scored live on the Mighty Allen Theatre Organ by house organist Ron Carter.
events.description_full: F.W. Murnau's silent classic follows a farmer torn between a seductive woman from the city and the wife he has left waiting at home. It won at the very first Academy Awards and is still counted among the most visually inventive films ever made. House organist Ron Carter accompanies live on the Strand Theatre's Mighty Allen, a four-manual digital replica of a 1920s Wurlitzer complete with a toy counter of train whistles, car horns, and gunshots. An organ pops concert begins thirty minutes before showtime; the film runs 97 minutes.

events.name: Improv Workshop
events.description_short: Free hands-on improv workshop from the Southside Sidekicks, first of a Sunday series that ends in a showcase.
events.description_full: The Southside Sidekicks, the resident improv troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, open their toolkit to anyone who wants to learn the form — games, scene work, and the confidence to say yes-and out loud. The workshops are free and family-friendly, and the series runs Sundays through September 6. Participants are invited to perform before a real audience in a showcase on September 13. Register at sidekicks@stgplays.com.

events.name: Ferris Bueller's Day Off (1986)
events.description_short: John Hughes's 1986 comedy of epic truancy, with a Mighty Allen Theatre Organ concert beforehand.
events.description_full: John Hughes's 1986 comedy about Ferris Bueller (Matthew Broderick), a high school senior who fakes sick and spends one glorious day loose in Chicago with his girlfriend, his anxious best friend, and a borrowed 1961 Ferrari, while his dean hunts him down. Ferris narrates straight to the camera the whole way. The house Mighty Allen Theatre Organ plays a half-hour concert before the film starts. 1 hour 43 minutes, rated PG-13.

events.name: Holy Chicken Sandwich
events.description_short: A new Atlanta comedy: 100 fast-food faithful camp overnight for free combos, and something goes wrong.
events.description_full: A new comedy from Atlanta playwright Kira Rockwell (Oh, To Be Pure Again): on the eve of a beloved fast-food chain's grand opening, a hundred devotees camp overnight in the parking lot to win a year of free large combos. Someone among them is stirring up trouble, and Jade, the brand-new store manager, has to root out the saboteur before her flock descends into chaos. Lisa Adler directs for Horizon Theatre. Runs through September 20.

events.name: Annie
events.description_short: Strouse and Charnin's 1977 Tony-winning orphan-meets-billionaire musical, opening Aurora Theatre's 31st season.
events.description_full: The 1977 Tony winner for Best Musical, drawn from Harold Gray's comic strip. In Depression-era New York, eleven-year-old Annie escapes Miss Hannigan's orphanage, takes up with a stray dog she names Sandy, and lands a Christmas invitation to billionaire Oliver Warbucks's mansion, all while holding out hope that her parents will come back for her. Charles Strouse and Martin Charnin's songs include "Tomorrow" and "It's the Hard Knock Life." Bethany Irby Okie directs for Aurora Theatre, with company co-founder Ann-Carol Pence leading the music. Runs through September 13.

events.name: Arsenic and Old Lace
events.description_short: Kesselring's black comedy: two sweet aunts, a body in the window seat, and elderberry wine best declined.
events.description_full: Joseph Kesselring's 1941 farce, later the Frank Capra film with Cary Grant. Drama critic Mortimer Brewster is about to announce his engagement when he finds a corpse in his elderly aunts' window seat, and the ladies are entirely unbothered by it. Their elderberry wine, it turns out, carries more than a hint of arsenic. Also in the house: a brother convinced he is Teddy Roosevelt and a fugitive sibling with a face rebuilt by a shady plastic surgeon. From the Henry Players, a volunteer community theatre company.

events.name: Comedy at the Den
events.description_short: A stand-up showcase in a deliberately small room, hosted by Atlanta comedian Jessica "It's All Good."
events.description_full: A lineup of stand-up comics with distinct styles, hosted by Atlanta comedian Jessica "It's All Good," known for sharp observations and a warm hand on the mic. The room is intentionally small, with limited seats and almost no distance between you and the performers. Presented by Out of Box Theatre.

events.name: Twelfth Night
events.description_short: Shakespeare's shipwreck comedy: twins separated at sea, a woman in disguise, and a love triangle nobody can untangle.
events.description_full: Shakespeare's comedy of mistaken identity. Shipwrecked and separated from her twin brother, Viola disguises herself as a young man and enters Duke Orsino's service, only to fall for him while carrying his love letters to a countess who promptly falls for her. Meanwhile a forged letter and a pair of yellow stockings send the pompous steward Malvolio into humiliating raptures. From Pumphouse Players, one of Georgia's oldest continuously operating community theatre companies.

events.name: Mamma Mia! and Margaritas!
events.description_short: The 2008 ABBA movie musical on the big screen, with a margarita bar and a pre-show organ concert.
events.description_full: The 2008 film of the ABBA jukebox musical: on a Greek island, a bride-to-be finds three men in her mother's old diary who might be her father, and quietly adds all of them to the guest list — mom Donna (Meryl Streep) none the wiser. Pierce Brosnan, Colin Firth, and Stellan Skarsgård are the candidates. The Strand Theatre sells margaritas, frozen or on the rocks, and boas are encouraged. A live concert on the house's Mighty Allen Theatre Organ starts thirty minutes before the film. 1 hour 48 minutes, rated PG-13.

events.name: Fox Archives Day
events.description_short: The Fox opens its archives — artifacts and archival materials from the 1929 movie palace, with Atlanta History Center.
events.description_full: The fifth annual open house of the Fox Theatre's archives, presented with the Atlanta History Center. Archival materials, artifacts, and exhibits trace the building's story, from 1929 movie palace to the grassroots campaign that saved it from demolition in the 1970s. The displays fill the Grand Salon and Egyptian Ballroom, so browsing doubles as a look inside two of the theatre's ornate event spaces.

events.name: Purpose
events.description_short: Branden Jacobs-Jenkins' Tony- and Pulitzer-winning drama of a Black political dynasty's reunion coming undone.
events.description_full: The Jaspers are a Chicago dynasty of ministers, activists, and changemakers, gathering for a long-overdue family reunion. When a young outsider arrives with questions nobody wants asked, the family's carefully guarded secrets start to surface over one evening. Branden Jacobs-Jenkins (Appropriate, An Octoroon) won both the 2025 Tony Award for Best Play and the Pulitzer Prize for Drama for this one; the New York Post called it "unstoppably fierce, funny, and ruthless." Keith Arthur Bolden directs for the Alliance Theatre, in a co-production with McCarter Theatre Center; runs through September 20.

events.name: On Golden Pond
events.description_short: Ernest Thompson's comedy-drama of a long marriage, a prickly father-daughter reunion, and one Maine summer.
events.description_full: Ernest Thompson's play about Ethel and Norman Thayer, spending their 48th summer at their lake house in the Maine woods as Norman turns 80 and his memory starts slipping into cantankerously morbid jokes. The birthday brings their daughter Chelsea, her boyfriend, and his teenage son, stirring up old grievances between father and daughter while Norman finds an unexpected fishing partner in the boy. Best known from the 1981 film with Henry Fonda and Katharine Hepburn. From Players Guild at Sugar Hill.

events.name: Mary Poppins (1964)
events.description_short: Disney's 1964 classic with Julie Andrews, preceded by a live concert on the Mighty Allen Theatre Organ.
events.description_full: The 1964 Disney musical that made Julie Andrews a movie star: a practically perfect nanny descends on the Banks children of Edwardian London, with Dick Van Dyke's chimney sweep Bert along for "Chim Chim Cher-ee," "A Spoonful of Sugar," and the rest. Rated G, 2 hours 19 minutes. A live pre-show concert on the house's Mighty Allen Theatre Organ starts thirty minutes before the film. Note: a concurrent Glover Park Concert closes North and East Park Streets, so parking in the Waddell deck is advised.

events.name: Hamilton
events.description_short: Lin-Manuel Miranda's hip-hop biography of Alexander Hamilton, on national tour.
events.description_full: Lin-Manuel Miranda's musical about Alexander Hamilton, the orphaned immigrant who wrote his way into the American Revolution and the founding of the nation. The score moves through hip-hop, R&B, and jazz, drawing on Ron Chernow's biography for its history. Winner of 11 Tony Awards and the Pulitzer Prize for Drama, with direction by Thomas Kail and choreography by Andy Blankenbuehler. A national touring production.

events.name: Parade
events.description_short: Uhry and Brown's Tony-winning musical of the 1913 Leo Frank case, a history that ended in Marietta itself.
events.description_full: Alfred Uhry and Jason Robert Brown's musical dramatization of the Leo Frank case: a Jewish factory manager from Brooklyn, convicted in 1913 of murdering thirteen-year-old worker Mary Phagan while an ambitious prosecutor and a sensation-hungry press fanned the flames. At the show's center is Frank's marriage, as his wife Lucille grows from distant spouse into his fiercest advocate. The original won Tony Awards for Uhry's book and Brown's score, and the 2023 Broadway revival won Best Revival of a Musical. The history it dramatizes ended with Frank's 1915 lynching in Marietta, the very town where this performance is staged.

events.name: The Philadelphia Story
events.description_short: Philip Barry's 1939 high-society comedy: a socialite's second wedding, crashed by her ex-husband and two tabloid journalists.
events.description_full: Philip Barry's 1939 comedy of the privileged class. Tracy Lord, headstrong daughter of the Philadelphia Lords, is set to marry a successful young snob when her ex-husband and a pair of tabloid journalists descend on the family estate for the wedding weekend. Barry wrote the role for Katharine Hepburn, who backed the play with her own money and carried it into the 1940 film with Cary Grant and James Stewart. Darci Rose Wells directs for Lionheart Theatre, where every ticket includes dessert at intermission; runs through September 27.

events.name: Ordinary Days
events.description_short: Adam Gwon's sung-through musical about four New Yorkers chasing fulfillment, love, and the occasional cab.
events.description_full: Adam Gwon's sung-through chamber musical follows four New Yorkers — Claire, Jason, Warren, and Deb — whose ordinary lives keep intersecting in a city of 8.3 million stories. Across 21 songs, they hunt for fulfillment, love, and cabs, and find their small moments adding up in unexpected ways. Presented by Arcadia Theater, the Alpharetta community company.

events.name: The Only Light in Reno
events.description_short: Topher Payne's play imagines Marilyn Monroe and Elizabeth Taylor alone together in a blacked-out Reno hotel, 1960.
events.description_full: August 1960: a forest fire has knocked out the power, and in a darkened hotel Marilyn Monroe and Elizabeth Taylor find themselves alone in the same room for the only time in their lives. In Atlanta playwright Topher Payne's telling, it does not go well. DeWayne Morgan directs for Onstage Atlanta. Runs through October 4.

events.name: Measure for Measure
events.description_short: Shakespeare's problem play: a disguised duke, a puritan deputy, and a novice nun bargaining for her brother's life.
events.description_full: The Duke of Vienna orders his city's dormant morality laws enforced, hands power to his strict deputy Angelo, and pretends to leave town. In truth he stays, disguised as a friar, to watch what his deputy does with the job. What Angelo does is sentence young Claudio to death for fornication, and when Claudio's sister Isabella, a novice nun, comes to plead for mercy, he names a price she cannot pay with a clear conscience. The First Folio filed it with the comedies, but its executions and moral bargaining have long earned it the label of problem play. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed with tavern table seating and food and drink service.

events.name: Improv Workshop Grad Show
events.description_short: Improv workshop graduates take the stage, building scenes and games from audience suggestions.
events.description_full: A graduation show for an improv comedy workshop: the students perform the games and scenes they've been practicing, with the audience supplying the suggestions that set each one in motion. Nothing scripted, everything made up on the spot. Presented by Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: Fences
events.description_short: August Wilson's Pulitzer winner: a former Negro League star in 1950s Pittsburgh, at war with his son's dreams.
events.description_full: August Wilson's 1985 drama, sixth in his ten-play Pittsburgh Cycle and winner of the Pulitzer Prize and the Tony for Best Play. Troy Maxson was a Negro League baseball star before the color barrier broke; now he hauls trash, builds a fence in his yard, and refuses to let his son chase a college football scholarship, convinced the game will break the boy the way it broke him. Theatrical Outfit produces in partnership with Dominion Entertainment Group and the Alabama Shakespeare Festival, a two-state revival of the play.

events.name: Wait Until Dark
events.description_short: Frederick Knott's 1966 thriller: a blind woman, three con men, and a doll worth killing for.
events.description_full: Frederick Knott's 1966 suspense thriller, the same one Audrey Hepburn filmed a year later. Susy Hendrix is blind, alone in her apartment, and the target of con artists who believe a missing doll is hidden there. As their schemes tighten around her, she has only her wits and her ears to work with. A revival from Cherokee Theatre, brought back by patron request; recommended for ages 12 and up.

events.name: Shrek (2001)
events.description_short: DreamWorks' 2001 fairy-tale spoof on the big screen — Mike Myers' ogre, Eddie Murphy's Donkey, 89 minutes, PG.
events.description_full: The 2001 DreamWorks comedy that won the first-ever Oscar for Best Animated Feature, back on a big screen. An ogre who just wants his swamp to himself strikes a deal with the fussy Lord Farquaad: rescue Princess Fiona, get the fairy-tale squatters off his land. Mike Myers voices Shrek in the Scottish accent he insisted on re-recording every line to get, with Eddie Murphy, Cameron Diaz, and John Lithgow rounding out the cast, plus a Smash Mouth-heavy soundtrack that dates it precisely and fondly. Loosely based on William Steig's picture book. 89 minutes, rated PG.

events.name: UNDERTALE: The Determination Symphony
events.description_short: An instrumental concert presented by Fox Theatre at Fox Theatre on Fri Sep 25, 8:00 PM ET.
events.description_full: Fox Theatre presents UNDERTALE: The Determination Symphony, an instrumental concert, at Fox Theatre on Fri Sep 25, 8:00 PM ET.

events.name: Sparrows (1926)
events.description_short: Mary Pickford's 1926 silent melodrama, screened with live accompaniment on the Mighty Allen Theatre Organ.
events.description_full: Mary Pickford plays Molly, the oldest of a band of orphans held captive on a baby farm deep in an alligator-infested swamp, who plots their escape when the children's cruel keeper decides to be rid of one of them. Part of the Strand Theatre's Silent Film Series, with house organist Ron Carter providing a live score on the Mighty Allen Theatre Organ, whose digital toy counter supplies whistles, thunder, and gunshots on cue. An organ pops concert begins thirty minutes before the film. Runs 1 hour 49 minutes; not rated.

events.name: i never asked for a gofundme
events.description_short: A world-premiere play: top surgery mistaken for breast cancer, and a small-town GoFundMe nobody asked for.
events.description_full: Millie and her partner Avery are riding out Avery's recovery from gender-affirming top surgery in Millie's hometown of Mobile, Alabama. Then a nosy evangelical family friend misreads the surgery as a breast-cancer fight and launches a GoFundMe on the couple's behalf, making them the talk of the town and the recipients of donations they never wanted. Whether to keep the money, and what keeping it would cost them, is the question. A world premiere from Actor's Express.

events.name: Mexodus
events.description_short: Two-performer hip-hop musical on the Underground Railroad's southern route into Mexico, with every song looped live onstage.
events.description_full: The Underground Railroad also ran south: thousands of enslaved people crossed the Rio Grande to Mexico, where slavery had already been abolished. Creators Brian Quijada and Nygel D. Robinson play every role and build each song's arrangement live on a looping station, following Henry, a man fleeing bondage in Texas, and Carlos, the Mexican stranger who becomes his ally. The New York Times called the Off-Broadway run "a dazzling showcase" for the sheer joy of music-making. David Mendizábal directs this Atlanta debut from the Alliance Theatre; runs through October 16.

events.name: Macbeth
events.description_short: Shakespeare's Scottish tragedy of witches, prophecy, and a couple who won't wait for fate, from the Atlanta Shakespeare Company.
events.description_full: Three witches hail a victorious Scottish general as a future king, and he and his wife decide not to leave the crown to chance. Shakespeare's shortest tragedy traces their climb from prophecy to murder to guilt that will not wash off. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed with food and drink served at your table. A 2025 Suzi Bass Awards Recommended Show.

events.name: Ripcord
events.description_short: David Lindsay-Abaire's comedy of two mismatched retirement-home roommates and the prank war that spirals between them.
events.description_full: David Lindsay-Abaire's comedy set in a shared room at the Bristol Place Senior Living Facility, where the cantankerous Abby wants her sunny upper-floor quarters to herself and the relentlessly cheerful new arrival Marilyn refuses to budge. What starts as a bet between the two women spirals into increasingly outrageous pranks, and each round unearths secrets both would prefer stayed buried. From Stage Door Theatre.

events.name: Indie Film Series 2026
events.description_short: Halloween edition of the monthly indie showcase: shorts and features from around the world, roughly 90 minutes.
events.description_full: A Halloween edition of the monthly indie film night, a compact festival packing short and feature-length independent films into a single sitting, about ninety minutes in all. Selections come from emerging and established filmmakers across the country and abroad, curated for originality and offbeat themes. From the Strand in partnership with the Cobb International Film Festival; screenings are open to the public.

events.name: The Fearless Gala 2026
events.description_short: Actor's Express marks 39 years with cabaret performances, silent and live auctions, and a tribute to Bruce Cohen.
events.description_full: Actor's Express celebrates its 39th year with a benefit built around cabaret performances from Atlanta theatre performers. The evening includes both a silent and a live auction, with food and drink from Endive Catering. Bruce Cohen is honored for his service and support across Atlanta theatre.

events.name: Like Father
events.description_short: New folk-pop musical: after her father's arrest, a teenager's family becomes true-crime podcast fodder.
events.description_full: A world-premiere coming-of-age musical written by Jacob Ryan Smith and Caroline Pernick, from Alliance Theatre. Seventeen-year-old Chris, reeling from her father's arrest, lands with the estranged mother and stepfather she barely knows, until a hit true-crime podcast drags the family's buried past into public view. The folk-pop score draws on the stories of relatives left behind by infamous killers, and on an industry that has grown to 250 million listeners. Tinashe Kajese-Bolden directs; the show won the first Open Jar Broadway Shark Tank and runs through November 15.

events.name: Other Paths To God
events.description_short: Five ICU nurses, $3 million missing, one scandal: a world-premiere comedy from Atlanta playwright Kelundra Smith.
events.description_full: A world premiere from Atlanta playwright Kelundra Smith, writer of The Wash. When $3 million goes missing at Morey Medical Center and fingers point at the ICU's five nurses, everybody's worst behavior comes out. Smith drew on interviews with real Atlanta nurses to write about what it means to give your life to a job that doesn't love you back. Commissioned through Horizon Theatre's Black Women Speak program; runs through November 8.

events.name: Crossroads & Cyphers
events.description_short: An immersive theatre-and-live-music work tracing rap's lineage from work songs and blues to hip-hop freestyle.
events.description_full: An immersive theatrical and live-music experience from Atlanta artists Alex Acosta and ANON The Griot, tracing the evolution of Black musical expression from enslaved African work songs and Southern blues to contemporary hip-hop freestyle. Built around two ideas — the crossroads and the cypher — it blends spoken word, live instrumentation, freestyle, and theatrical narrative to explore how music has long served as a tool for survival, resistance, healing, and community. Led by artists from Soul Food Cypher, the piece preserves cultural memory while introducing audiences to the historical roots and social significance of hip-hop.

events.name: Atlanta Ballet 2: Cinderella
events.description_short: A narrated, kid-friendly Cinderella ballet from Atlanta Ballet 2 — 75 minutes with intermission, ages 4 and up.
events.description_full: The glass-slipper fairy tale as one of Atlanta Ballet's Family Ballets: a short, narrated production from Atlanta Ballet 2, the company's second troupe, built so kids as young as four can follow the story. Bruce Wells choreographs, and a narrator walks young audiences through the plot as the dancers tell it. Runs an hour and fifteen minutes, including a 20-minute intermission.

events.name: Ride the Cyclone
events.description_short: Six choir kids die on a roller coaster and compete in song for one shot back at life.
events.description_full: Jacob Richmond and Brooke Maxwell's cult dark-comedy musical: six teenagers from a small-town Canadian chamber choir perish on a faulty coaster and wake in limbo, where a mechanical fortune teller offers exactly one of them a return to life. Each teen pleads their case in song, from autotuned gangsta rap to a torchy French lament. The show has local roots of a sort — its 2019 Alliance Theatre run supplied the cast for the world premiere recording. A community production from the Henry Players.

events.name: Frankenstein: The Monster Play
events.description_short: Mary Shelley's gothic tale onstage: a scientist, the creature he abandons, and the reckoning between them.
events.description_full: A stage adaptation of Mary Shelley's 1818 novel. Victor Frankenstein stitches life together from the dead, recoils from what he's made, and flees; the abandoned creature, left to learn the world alone, goes looking for his maker. From Pumphouse Players, one of Georgia's oldest continuously operating community theatres, performing in a 70-seat house where nobody sits far from the monster.

events.name: Cotton Patch Gospel: Season 50 Return Engagement
events.description_short: Matthew's Gospel retold in small-town Georgia with Harry Chapin's bluegrass score, back for Theatrical Outfit's 50th season.
events.description_full: Tom Key and Russell Treyz's musical transplants the Gospel of Matthew to rural Georgia, where Jesus is born in Gainesville and the storytelling runs on banjo and fiddle. The bluegrass score is by Harry Chapin, his final work before his death in 1981, and the show draws on Clarence Jordan's homespun Cotton Patch translations of the New Testament. It returns as a special engagement in Theatrical Outfit's 50th anniversary season, with performances through October 18.

events.name: Outlander in Concert
events.description_short: Bear McCreary's Outlander score played by a live orchestra, with the series' key scenes on a cinema-sized screen.
events.description_full: The officially licensed Outlander concert, created with showrunner Matthew B. Roberts and the show's Emmy-winning composer Bear McCreary as the series heads toward its 2026 conclusion. A live orchestra performs McCreary's score while the show's most stirring moments play on a cinema-sized screen, tracing Claire and Jamie's story from the standing stones of Craigh na Dun onward. The touring production premiered in Scotland and is licensed by Sony Pictures Television in collaboration with Starz. Recommended for ages 12 and up with parental guidance.

events.name: Hannah & Greta, or The Cost of Livin' The Dream
events.description_short: A loose Hansel-and-Gretel retelling: two new grads chase internet fame and a job that promises them everything.
events.description_full: A loose adaptation of Hansel and Gretel following two recent college grads and would-be internet-famous friends who fall for a job that promises everything — a decent paycheck, health insurance, and a chance to actually use their degrees. As the temptations pull them in, they're forced to ask how much of themselves they are willing to give up to live their dreams. A new work by Atlanta playwright Bobbi Twiggs, directed by Charis Sellick.

events.name: Superior Donuts
events.description_short: Tracy Letts's comedy-drama of a burned-out donut shop owner and the young writer who shakes him awake.
events.description_full: Tracy Letts, the Pulitzer winner behind August: Osage County, in a gentler mode: a former '60s radical presides over his fading donut shop in Chicago's Uptown until Franco, a bright young Black writer, turns up looking for work and full of plans to fix the place. Reviewing the 2008 Steppenwolf premiere, the New York Times's Charles Isherwood called it "insubstantial and sweet" yet "hard to dislike." Robby Myles directs for Authenticity Theater. Runs through October 31.

events.name: Cadillac Crew
events.description_short: Tori Sampson's drama: four activists in a Virginia civil rights office ask whether the movement's equality includes women.
events.description_full: Tori Sampson's play unfolds in a Virginia civil rights office on the day of a much-anticipated Rosa Parks speech, where four activists start asking whether the movement's promise of equality extends to women. It reclaims the stories of the overlooked organizers behind desegregation, with more humor than the setup suggests. Karlotta Washington directs for the Conyers Rockdale Council for the Arts. Runs through October 25.

events.name: Vanya and Sonia and Masha and Spike
events.description_short: Christopher Durang's Chekhov-riffing comedy: a movie-star sister, her young boyfriend, and a family farmhouse up for sale.
events.description_full: Christopher Durang's comedy borrows its names and its moods from Chekhov, then lets them loose in present-day Pennsylvania. Siblings Vanya and Sonia have spent their quiet lives in the family farmhouse; their movie-star sister Masha blows in for the weekend with her much younger boyfriend Spike and announces she's selling the place. Rivalry, regret, and a costume party follow. The play won the 2013 Tony Award for Best Play; this production comes from Arcadia Theater.

events.name: Sidekicks Show: Seriously Scary
events.description_short: Southside Theatre Guild's in-house improv troupe takes a swing at the scary — scenes built from audience suggestions.
events.description_full: The Southside Sidekicks, the in-house improv troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, perform a spooky-themed set — everything made up on the spot, shaped by whatever the audience throws at them. No script, no rewinds, and a different show every time by definition.

events.name: Sonic Live In Concert
events.description_short: Sonic the Hedgehog's game music played live — rock band, ensemble, and vocalists synced to footage from 35 years of games.
events.description_full: A concert built from three and a half decades of Sonic the Hedgehog music, from the Genesis-era chiptunes of Green Hill Zone to "Undefeatable," performed live by an ensemble with rock musicians, electronics, and vocalists while moments from the games play onscreen. The program draws heavily on the work of Jun Senoue and Tomoya Ohtani, the composers who shaped Sonic's sound across generations. The arrangements hide musical Easter eggs for longtime fans to catch mid-show.

events.name: Buena Vista Social Club
events.description_short: National touring production of the Broadway musical behind Cuba's landmark 1997 album, sung entirely in Spanish.
events.description_full: The Broadway musical built from the songs of the 1997 Buena Vista Social Club album: singer Omara Portuondo, once celebrated across Cuba as the "Queen of Feeling," has long vanished from the spotlight when a young record producer arrives with one more chance at the studio. The story moves between 1990s Havana and the musicians' younger selves in the 1950s, and the score — son, danzón, bolero — is performed entirely in Spanish. Book by Marco Ramirez, with choreography by Justin Peck and Patricia Delgado; the show won a Special Tony Award for its live onstage musicians and a Grammy for its cast album. A national touring production.

events.name: Jeffrey Hatcher's The Turn of the Screw
events.description_short: Two actors, one piano, and Henry James's ghost story performed in the round — no intermission, one week only.
events.description_full: Jeffrey Hatcher's adaptation of Henry James's 1898 novella: a new governess arrives at an English manor and begins seeing ghosts around the children in her care, including the specter of the woman who held the job before her. Whether the hauntings are real or her own fevered invention is the question the play keeps turning. Two actors carry the whole story, performed in the round with live atmospheric piano. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company; a one-week run with no intermission and no latecomer seating.

events.name: Support Local Theatre
events.description_short: A costumed murder-mystery party raising money for Merely Players, with clues unfolding all night.
events.description_full: Merely Players' fundraiser takes the shape of an interactive whodunit: guests arrive in costume and masks, clues surface as the night goes on, and anyone in the room can crack the case. Dogwood Social pours specialty cocktails, and a 50/50 raffle and silent auction round out the fundraising. Masks are sold on site for anyone who wants to add to the intrigue.

events.name: Miracle on 34th Street
events.description_short: The 1947 holiday classic on stage: a department-store Santa who insists he's the real thing.
events.description_full: A stage version of the 1947 film about Kris Kringle, an old man hired to play Santa at Macy's who calmly maintains that he is Santa — a claim that eventually lands him in a New York courtroom with his sanity on trial. Between the skeptical single mother who hired him and her even more skeptical young daughter, the question of believing becomes the whole ballgame. From Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: It's a Wonderful Life: A Live Radio Play
events.description_short: Capra's 1946 Christmas classic restaged as a live 1940s radio broadcast, with onstage foley effects and actors doubling dozens of roles.
events.description_full: The story of George Bailey, the Bedford Falls building-and-loan man who hits bottom one snowy Christmas Eve and gets shown what his town would look like if he'd never been born. This adaptation of Frank Capra's 1946 film plays out as a period radio broadcast: a small ensemble at vintage microphones voicing dozens of characters, with every footstep, door slam, and ringing bell made live by a foley artist in full view. Presented by The Strand Theatre.

events.name: Other Desert Cities
events.description_short: Jon Robin Baitz's Pulitzer-finalist family drama: a daughter's tell-all memoir detonates a Palm Springs Christmas.
events.description_full: Jon Robin Baitz's drama, a finalist for the 2012 Pulitzer Prize, unfolds over Christmas Eve 2004 in Palm Springs. Brooke Wyeth comes home after six years away with a finished memoir that digs into the death of her brother, a chapter her staunchly Republican parents have spent decades keeping closed. Her aunt Silda, fresh from rehab and firmly on the other side of the political fence, is also in the house. The festive reunion turns into a fight over memory, loyalty, and who gets to tell the family's story. Presented by Merely Players.

events.name: Miracle on 34th Street
events.description_short: The 1947 Christmas classic on stage: a department-store Santa who insists he is the real thing.
events.description_full: A stage adaptation of the 1947 holiday classic. When the Macy's Thanksgiving Parade Santa shows up drunk, a white-bearded old man named Kris Kringle steps in as a last-minute replacement — and turns out to be so convincing that every child believes him, not least because he claims to actually be Santa Claus. Along the way he works on the toughest cases in New York: a cynical, divorced single mother and her fairy-tale-proof six-year-old daughter. From the Players Guild at Sugar Hill, a Gwinnett County community theatre company.

events.name: Silent Night
events.description_short: Kevin Puts's Pulitzer-winning opera about the 1914 Christmas truce, sung in five languages.
events.description_full: Kevin Puts's first opera, with a libretto by Mark Campbell adapted from the film Joyeux Noël, dramatizes the real Christmas truce of 1914, when German, French, and Scottish soldiers put down their weapons and met in no-man's-land. A German tenor's carol, answered by a Scottish bagpipe, sparks the cease-fire; the generals back at headquarters are considerably less moved. The score earned Puts a Pulitzer in 2012. Sung in English, French, German, Italian, and Latin. From The Atlanta Opera; runs through November 15.

events.name: Othello
events.description_short: Shakespeare's tragedy of jealousy — Iago whispers, and a great general's faith in his wife unravels.
events.description_full: Shakespeare's tragedy of a Venetian general undone not by war but by his own ensign: Iago, passed over for promotion, sets out to convince Othello that his new wife Desdemona has been unfaithful, using little more than insinuation and a misplaced handkerchief. Iago is one of the longest roles in the canon, and watching him work the room is half the horror. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed Elizabethan-style with food and drink served at your table.

events.name: NieR: Orchestra Concert 12026 [YoRHa]!
events.description_short: Music from Square Enix's NieR games — full orchestra, chorus, original vocalists, and a new narrated Automata prequel story.
events.description_full: The music of the NieR video games performed live, presented by AWR Music Productions and Square Enix. Eric Roth conducts a full orchestra and chorus, joined by J'Nique Nicole and Emi Evans, the singers whose voices defined the original game recordings. Between the music, recorded narration from NieR:Automata's Japanese voice cast — including Yui Ishikawa as 2B — tells the story of the Pearl Harbor Descent Operation, the prequel to Automata, with visuals on a giant screen. A follow-up from the team behind the earlier NieR:Orchestra Concert [the end of data] tour.

events.name: A Christmas Carol
events.description_short: Dickens's Scrooge-and-three-ghosts classic in David H. Bell's adaptation with live music, from the Alliance Theatre.
events.description_full: Charles Dickens's 1843 tale of Ebenezer Scrooge, the miser visited by the ghosts of Christmas Past, Present, and Yet to Come on a single night of reckoning. The Alliance Theatre's annual production uses David H. Bell's adaptation, staged with live music and a large company of characters. Amanda Watkins directs, building on Leora Morris's original staging. Runs through December 27.

events.name: Mel Konner’s Sixteen Springs or Four Loves (Staged Reading)
events.description_short: A book-in-hand reading of a new play filling in the sixteen missing years of The Winter's Tale.
events.description_full: Atlanta playwright Mel Konner imagines what happens during the sixteen-year gap in Shakespeare's The Winter's Tale, and this time Antigonus survives the bear. The abandoned babe, here named Trouvita, grows up smart and strong in a shepherd family, while back in Sicilia a slandered queen and her mad king head toward very different fates. A staged reading from the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, ahead of a full production in Spring 2027.

events.name: Death Becomes Her
events.description_short: The Broadway musical of the 1992 camp classic: two frenemies, one youth potion, and a grudge built to outlast death.
events.description_full: The musical adapted from the 1992 Robert Zemeckis film: fading actress Madeline Ashton and long-suffering writer Helen Sharp have spent decades as frenemies, and the feud turns supernatural when a mysterious potion restores their youth and makes the grudge eternal. Book by Marco Pennette, with songs by Julia Mattison and Noel Carey. Tony winner Christopher Gattelli directs and choreographs. Variety called it "a laugh-filled, tuneful musical to die for."

events.name: The Last Gift
events.description_short: World-premiere Christmas play about a crotchety nursing-home resident and the young child who wins him over.
events.description_full: A new play set in a nursing home around the Christmas holidays, where a young child works on the defenses of a crotchety old man. Atlanta playwright Earnest Lewis, also a children's book author, wrote it, weaving in elder care, adoption, loneliness, and family. Dave Di Pietro directs this world premiere for the Conyers Rockdale Council for the Arts. Runs through November 22.

events.name: At All Cost
events.description_short: A drama about Black WWII veterans denied their promised GI Bill benefits, presented by Conyers Rockdale Council for the Arts.
events.description_full: A drama following a Black soldier home from World War II, where the GI Bill's promises of housing, education, and opportunity quietly evaporate at the point of delivery. The play traces how that denial rippled through generations of Black families in America. Presented by the Conyers Rockdale Council for the Arts, with artistic director Rashida Costa among the cast.

events.name: All is Calm: The Christmas Truce of 1914
events.description_short: Peter Rothstein's a cappella retelling of the WWI Christmas truce, built from soldiers' letters and period songs.
events.description_full: On Christmas Eve 1914, soldiers along the Western Front laid down their rifles, crossed no-man's land, and sang together. Peter Rothstein's documentary-style musical tells that true story through trench songs, patriotic tunes, and carols, sung a cappella and woven with the soldiers' own words from letters and journals. Music by Erick Lichte and Timothy C. Takach. Presented by The Atlanta Opera as part of its Discoveries series.

events.name: It's a Wonderful Life: A Live Radio Play
events.description_short: Joe Landry's radio-play staging of the 1946 Capra classic, complete with live foley sound effects and music.
events.description_full: Joe Landry's adaptation reframes the 1946 Frank Capra film as a 1940s radio broadcast: a small ensemble of actors plays every citizen of Bedford Falls, with sound effects and music made live on stage. The story is the one you know from the movie, where George Bailey hits bottom on Christmas Eve and an angel shows him what the town would look like if he had never been born. From Merely Players, and family-friendly.

events.name: A Christmas Story: The Musical
events.description_short: Ralphie's quest for a Red Ryder BB gun, sung: the Pasek and Paul musical of the 1983 holiday film.
events.description_full: The 1983 movie that runs on a loop every December, turned Broadway musical with songs by Benj Pasek and Justin Paul (later of Dear Evan Hansen) and a book by Joseph Robinette. Nine-year-old Ralphie Parker schemes toward the one gift every adult swears will shoot his eye out: an Official Red Ryder carbine-action BB gun. The leg lamp, the tongue on the flagpole, and the pink bunny suit all make it onstage, in a score The New York Times called "likable" and "perky." From Arcadia Theater.

events.name: Home for the Holidays: A Henry Players Revue
events.description_short: A cabaret-style holiday revue with community performers of all ages — songs, dance, comedy skits, and spoken word.
events.description_full: A holiday revue in cabaret style, back for its second year, with community members of all ages taking the stage themselves. Expect a grab bag of seasonal acts: singing, dancing, comedy skits, and spoken word. From the Henry Players, the volunteer community theatre company that has served Henry County since 1991.

events.name: A Dickens Christmas
events.description_short: A farce about a theatre troupe's disastrous attempt to stage A Christmas Carol, back for a second year.
events.description_full: Not A Christmas Carol itself, but a comedy about trying to perform it: an earnest theatre troupe takes on the Dickens classic and everything that can go wrong does, from blown cues to full-scale onstage disasters. The holiday warmth survives the wreckage, more or less. From Cherokee Theatre, returning for a second year, and suitable for all ages.

events.name: Just in the Nick of Time
events.description_short: A play from Pumphouse Players, staged in their 70-seat converted 1940s cinema.
events.description_full: A play from Pumphouse Players, one of Georgia's oldest continuously operating community theatre companies. The company performs in a converted 1940s cinema with just 70 seats, so no one watches from a distance.

events.name: Charles Dickens' A Christmas Carol
events.description_short: Dickens' ghost story told by a handful of actors, with song, an Atlanta Shakespeare Company holiday tradition of 20-plus years.
events.description_full: J. Tony Brown's adaptation of Dickens' 1843 novella, a fixture of the Atlanta Shakespeare Company's December calendar for more than two decades, this year in a staging by Kati Grace Kirby. The approach is storytelling rather than spectacle: a small group of performers, with a bit of song, follows the miser Ebenezer Scrooge from his counting house through a Christmas Eve visited by three ghosts and a certain small boy named Tiny Tim. The playhouse's dinner-table seating means food and drink arrive during the performance.

events.name: Winter Wonderettes
events.description_short: Roger Bean's holiday sequel to The Marvelous Wonderettes: girl-group harmonies at a hardware store party, and Santa's gone missing.
events.description_full: A holiday installment of Roger Bean's Marvelous Wonderettes jukebox musicals, with the four girls in tight harmony providing the entertainment at Harper's Hardware annual holiday party. When Santa turns up missing, they improvise their way to saving the night. The setlist leans on seasonal standards like "Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town," "Jingle Bell Rock," and "Run, Rudolph, Run." From Stage Door Theatre.

events.name: Sketch Comedy Show
events.description_short: A night of short comic scenes from the all-volunteer Southside Theatre Guild.
events.description_full: Sketch comedy in the classic mold: short scenes, quick blackouts, and a new premise every few minutes, performed live by members of Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: Sidekicks Show: Naughty or Nice List
events.description_short: Holiday-flavored improv from Southside Sidekicks, the Guild's in-house comedy troupe — scenes made up on the spot.
events.description_full: Improv comedy from the Southside Sidekicks, the in-house troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, this time with a naughty-or-nice seasonal spin. Nothing is scripted: the performers build scenes and characters from audience suggestions, so the show that happens is the one the room helps make.

events.name: A Christmas Tradition
events.description_short: An annual holiday song-and-dance revue — Christmas favorites from carols to the funky, in full costume.
events.description_full: The Strand Theatre's own annual holiday revue: a company of costumed singers and dancers working through Christmas favorites both traditional and funky. Fast-moving, family-friendly, and built as a hometown seasonal fixture rather than a touring package.

events.name: Sidekicks Show: A Holly Jolly Murder Mystery
events.description_short: An improvised holiday-themed murder mystery from Southside Theatre Guild's in-house improv troupe, the Southside Sidekicks.
events.description_full: The Southside Sidekicks, the resident improv troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, spin a murder mystery with a holiday wrapping — built on the spot, no script, shaped by whatever the audience throws at them. Nobody in the cast knows whodunit either until the night decides.

events.name: Holiday at Herren's: 50th Season Special
events.description_short: A one-night holiday cabaret from Theatrical Outfit, part of the company's 50th-anniversary season.
events.description_full: A one-night holiday cabaret marking Theatrical Outfit's 50th season. Holiday songs delivered up close, in cabaret's small-room style, from a company that has been producing in Atlanta since 1977.

events.name: Mrs. Doubtfire
events.description_short: The 1993 Robin Williams film as a Broadway musical: divorced dad, Scottish nanny disguise, national touring production.
events.description_full: The musical adaptation of the 1993 film: Daniel Hillard, an out-of-work actor who loses custody of his kids in a messy divorce, invents Euphegenia Doubtfire, a kindly Scottish nanny, to stay in their lives. Songs come from Wayne and Karey Kirkpatrick, the songwriting team behind Something Rotten!, with Karey Kirkpatrick and John O'Farrell sharing book duties. Four-time Tony winner Jerry Zaks directs. A national touring production, presented as part of the Broadway in Atlanta series.

events.name: NUTCRACKER! Magical Christmas Ballet
events.description_short: Tchaikovsky's Nutcracker as touring spectacle: international dancers, acrobatics, and towering puppets.
events.description_full: The familiar Nutcracker story, set to Tchaikovsky's score: Clara, a Christmas Eve gift, and a dream that carries her into a candy-bright kingdom. This touring production bills itself as North America's largest Nutcracker, with an international cast, acrobatics, and towering puppets sharing the stage.

events.name: A Whole Weekend of Out of a Hat
events.description_short: Shakespeare roulette: the play and every role drawn from a hat minutes before curtain.
events.description_full: The Atlanta Shakespeare Company's resident actors perform a full Shakespeare play without knowing which one until moments before the show — the title comes out of a hat, and roles are assigned two minutes to curtain. Part improv, mostly Shakespeare, entirely dependent on nerve and a deep memory for verse. Performed tavern-style, with table seating and food and drink service during the show. These Out of a Hat nights tend to sell out.

events.name: BOOP! The Musical
events.description_short: Betty Boop trades her black-and-white cartoon world for present-day New York in this touring Broadway musical comedy.
events.description_full: Betty Boop, nearly a century into cartoon stardom, wants one ordinary day off. Instead, Grampy's teleporter drops her out of her black-and-white world and into present-day New York, where full color, a jazz musician named Dwayne, and a Comic Con crowd that already knows her face are waiting. Jerry Mitchell (Kinky Boots, Hairspray) directs and choreographs, with a score by pop hitmaker David Foster and a book by Bob Martin (The Drowsy Chaperone). A national touring production of the musical that ran on Broadway in 2025.

events.name: Pride & Prejudice
events.description_short: Austen's comedy of courtship in a fast, playful adaptation — Lizzy Bennet swears off marriage; Darcy keeps turning up.
events.description_full: Jane Austen's 1813 comedy of courtship, here in a loose, high-energy adaptation that treats the marriage plot as a game with real stakes. Lizzy Bennet has decided she will never marry. Then Mr. Darcy — aggravating, and only vaguely handsome by her account — keeps crossing her path. Presented by Arcadia Theater, the Alpharetta community theatre.

events.name: Much Ado About Nothing
events.description_short: Shakespeare's Beatrice and Benedick trade insults until their friends trick them into love, from the Atlanta Shakespeare Company.
events.description_full: Shakespeare's comedy of two committed singles: Beatrice and Benedick spar so relentlessly that their friends conspire to make each believe the other is secretly in love. Meanwhile the villainous Don John schemes to wreck the wedding of the young lovers Claudio and Hero. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed Elizabethan-style with table seating and food and drink service during the show. A 2026 Suzi Bass Awards Recommended Show.

events.name: Sidekicks Show: Choose Your Own (Improv) Adventure!
events.description_short: Unscripted comedy from the Southside Sidekicks, built on the spot from audience suggestions.
events.description_full: Nothing is scripted: the Southside Sidekicks make the scenes up as they go, steered by whatever the audience throws at them, and the title suggests the crowd gets a say in where the night wanders. The Sidekicks are the in-house improv troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: Miseducated
events.description_short: New play from Brandon P. Fleming's memoir: Atlanta debate students, and a coach arguing with the universe itself.
events.description_full: Rudi Goblen adapts Brandon P. Fleming's memoir into a world premiere commissioned by the Alliance Theatre. Atlanta high schoolers train for an elite Harvard debate program under a coach who demands they sharpen every argument. Then Brandon gets pulled into a surreal debate of his own, arguing with the universe over the memories that made him and whether a life can be rewritten. Teo Castellanos directs, with the story told through debate rounds, music, and spoken word. Runs through February 21.

events.name: 12 Angry Jurors
events.description_short: Reginald Rose's jury-room drama: eleven guilty votes, one holdout, and a murder verdict hinging on reasonable doubt.
events.description_full: Reginald Rose's classic deliberation drama, here in the retitled version that opens the jury room to a cast of any gender. A teenager stands accused of murdering his father, and the first vote comes back eleven to one for guilty. The lone holdout wants to talk it through, and one hot afternoon in one room, the case starts to come apart. From Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: Carmen
events.description_short: Bizet's tragedy of obsession: a soldier, a bullfighter, and the woman who refuses to be owned.
events.description_full: Georges Bizet's 1875 opera, set in Seville: Carmen, a factory worker who answers to no one, throws a flower at the one soldier ignoring her. Don José picks it up and unravels — deserting the army, abandoning his sweetheart, and following Carmen into the mountains, only to lose her to the bullfighter Escamillo. The score holds some of opera's most familiar melodies, the Habanera and the Toreador Song among them, though its first Paris audiences were scandalized by so much lawlessness on the Opéra-Comique stage. From The Atlanta Opera.

events.name: Water For Elephants
events.description_short: Sara Gruen's Depression-era circus novel as a musical, on national tour after premiering in Atlanta before Broadway.
events.description_full: During the Depression, a young man who has just lost everything hops a moving train and lands among the Benzini Brothers circus, where his veterinary training, an untrainable elephant named Rosie, and the ringmaster's wife reshape his life, all remembered through the eyes of his older self. Rick Elice adapted the book from Sara Gruen's 2006 novel, with a score by PigPen Theatre Co. and Rosie brought to life through puppetry. The show premiered in Atlanta at the Alliance Theatre in 2023 before a Broadway run that earned seven Tony nominations, including Best Musical. This is the national touring production.

events.name: Sweat
events.description_short: Lynn Nottage's Pulitzer-winning drama of factory friends in a Rust Belt bar as the layoffs begin.
events.description_full: Lynn Nottage's 2015 drama, winner of the 2017 Pulitzer Prize, set mostly in a bar in Reading, Pennsylvania, where a circle of steel-plant workers has gathered for years. When one of the friends is promoted to management and jobs start moving to Mexico, the lines between labor and boss, and along race, begin to cut through the group. Nottage built the play from years of interviews with residents of Reading, then one of the poorest cities in America. From the Henry Players.

events.name: Little Women
events.description_short: Alcott's four March sisters grow up, in Thomas Hischak's stage adaptation from Players Guild at Sugar Hill.
events.description_full: Louisa May Alcott's novel of the four March sisters — headstrong Jo, practical Meg, gentle Beth, and ambitious Amy — coming of age in Civil War-era New England, each chasing a different idea of what her life should be. Thomas Hischak's stage adaptation, from Players Guild at Sugar Hill.

events.name: The Romance Repertory: Romeo and Juliet and Much Ado About Nothing
events.description_short: This performance is Romeo and Juliet, half of a repertory pairing Shakespeare's tragedy with Much Ado About Nothing.
events.description_full: Two Shakespeare plays about love, running in repertory from the Atlanta Shakespeare Company. This performance is Romeo and Juliet: two teenagers fall for each other across a family feud, with one well-intentioned Friar trying to hold it all together. Kelly Criss directs the company's 23rd anniversary production of the tragedy.
