On alternate dates, Alejandra Ruiz directs Much Ado About Nothing, the Beatrice-and-Benedick comedy of two confirmed singles too witty for their own good. Table seating with food and drink service during the show; the repertory runs through February 28.

events.name: Proof
events.description_short: David Auburn's Pulitzer-winning drama: a mathematician's daughter, a mysterious proof, and the question of who wrote it.
events.description_full: David Auburn's drama won the 2001 Pulitzer Prize and the Tony for Best Play. Catherine spent years caring for her father, a brilliant University of Chicago mathematician whose mind unraveled before his death. Now one of his former students is combing through the hundred-odd notebooks he left behind, and a groundbreaking proof about prime numbers surfaces. The question of who wrote it forces Catherine to reckon with how much of her father, genius and instability alike, she may have inherited. From Stage Door Theatre.

events.name: Time Stands Still
events.description_short: Donald Margulies's drama of a war photographer home from Iraq, deciding whether she can want an ordinary life.
events.description_full: Sarah, a photojournalist, is back in Brooklyn after a roadside bomb in Iraq nearly killed her. Her boyfriend James, a reporter who left the assignment before it happened, carries the guilt. A visit from an old friend and his much younger new girlfriend sharpens the question of whether the couple can trade the front lines for something conventional. Donald Margulies's play earned a Tony nomination for Best Play, with the New York Daily News praising its "intelligence and humor." From Pumphouse Players, staged in a 70-seat converted 1940s cinema.

events.name: LETTERKENNY LIVE
events.description_short: The Letterkenny cast live: all-new sketches from Wayne, Squirrely Dan, and the rest of the hicks, skids, and hockey players.
events.description_full: A national touring live show from the cast of Letterkenny, Jared Keeso's Canadian sitcom about the hicks, skids, and hockey players of a small rural Ontario town, famous for its rapid-fire small-town vernacular. Keeso appears as Wayne alongside original castmates including Nathan Dales and K. Trevor Wilson, performing sketch material written for the tour rather than recycled from the show. The series ran twelve seasons on Crave; Rolling Stone called it "marvelously goofy."

events.name: Stage Kiss
events.description_short: Sarah Ruhl's comedy: two exes cast as lovers in a creaky 1930s melodrama, and the kissing gets complicated.
events.description_full: Sarah Ruhl's backstage comedy about two actors with a shared romantic past who land opposite each other as the leads in a forgotten 1930s melodrama. The script requires them to kiss, repeatedly, and the line between playing lovers and being them starts to dissolve. A community production from Arcadia Theater.

events.name: Alice in Neverland
events.description_short: A world-premiere musical: twenty years on, a disenchanted grown Alice tries to find Wonderland again — and lands in Neverland instead.
events.description_full: A new Alliance musical from Phil Kenny and Reston Williams. Two decades after the rabbit hole, Alice's life has come apart; chasing the happiness she remembers, she sets out for Wonderland and crash-lands somewhere far more dangerous. A story about reclaiming childhood and what it costs to never grow up.

events.name: Sidekicks Show: The Improv School of Witchcraft & Wizardry
events.description_short: Wizard-school-themed improv night from the Southside Sidekicks, Southside Theatre Guild's in-house comedy troupe.
events.description_full: The Southside Sidekicks, Southside Theatre Guild's in-house improv troupe, take the wizard-school premise as their playground for a night of scenes and games built on the spot from audience suggestions. Nothing is scripted, so what happens depends on what the room throws at them.

events.name: What the Constitution Means to Me
events.description_short: Heidi Schreck's Tony-nominated play revisiting her teenage Constitution-debate speeches, in its Atlanta premiere.
events.description_full: Heidi Schreck put herself through college giving prize-winning speeches about the Constitution at American Legion halls; her 2019 Broadway play returns to those contests, toggling between her fifteen-year-old self and the woman she became. What starts as a debate exercise opens into the stories of the women in her family and how the document actually treated them — funny, personal, and pointed by turns. A Tony nominee for Best Play and a Pulitzer finalist, it gets its Atlanta premiere from Theatrical Outfit.

events.name: The Women of Willow Creek
events.description_short: Six women, 28 days, one rehabilitation facility: a drama of recovery from Merely Players.
events.description_full: A drama that follows Harper and five other women through a 28-day stay in a rehabilitation facility, their recoveries tangled together as the days count down. The subject matter is heavy and unflinching — suicide, eating disorders, self-harm, substance use, and PTSD all figure in — but the play finds real humor in the darkest corners of the ward. From Merely Players, a small non-equity company that stages work reflecting its community's diversity.

events.name: Shakespeare Out of a Hat: Romeo and Juliet
events.description_short: Romeo and Juliet with roles drawn from a hat two minutes before curtain — and a happy ending.
events.description_full: The play is set, but the casting isn't: the Atlanta Shakespeare Company's actors pull their roles out of a hat moments before the show starts, so nobody knows who's playing Romeo, Juliet, or the Nurse until it's underway. Part improv, part Shakespeare, and this version promises the star-crossed lovers a happy ending. A one-night-only special event, performed with the company's usual table seating and food and drink service.

events.name: Black Comedy
events.description_short: Peter Shaffer's 1965 farce with the lighting reversed: when the power fails, the stage lights up.
events.description_full: Peter Shaffer's one-act farce runs on a single inspired gimmick: light and dark are swapped, so when the power blows, the stage blazes and the audience watches everyone grope blindly through the chaos. A young sculptor has borrowed his absent neighbor's antiques to impress a millionaire art collector, and the blackout brings the neighbor home early, along with an ex-mistress and a string of unexpected guests. One Guardian critic called the original 'an uproarious piece of slapstick vaudeville.' A Cherokee Theatre production, recommended for ages 12 and up.

events.name: Julius Caesar
events.description_short: Shakespeare's tragedy of conspiracy and assassination in Rome, from the Atlanta Shakespeare Company.
events.description_full: Shakespeare's 1599 tragedy of the plot against Julius Caesar: senators convinced they are saving the republic, a murder on the Senate floor, and Marc Antony's funeral oration that turns the city against them. The play's real center is Brutus, torn between loyalty to his friend and fear of what Caesar might become. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, with artistic director Jeff Watkins playing Caesar and Kenneth Wigley as Antony, performed in the company's Elizabethan-style playhouse with table seating and food and drink service during the show.

events.name: The Flying Dutchman
events.description_short: Wagner's ghost-ship opera: a captain cursed to sail forever, ashore once every seven years seeking love.
events.description_full: Richard Wagner's early opera built on the old maritime legend of a phantom ship doomed never to make port. Its captain is allowed ashore once every seven years to search for a woman whose faithful love can break the curse, and in the sea captain's daughter Senta he may have found her. Wagner's storm-tossed score made this his first enduring success. Presented by the Atlanta Opera.

events.name: Shakespeare Out of a Hat: Julius Caesar
events.description_short: Improv meets Shakespeare: actors draw their Julius Caesar roles from a hat two minutes before the show begins.
events.description_full: The Atlanta Shakespeare Company's classically trained actors know the script; nobody knows who's playing whom until roles come out of a hat moments before curtain. The play in question is Julius Caesar, the conspiracy drama that made the Ides of March infamous, so Brutus could be anyone. A one-night-only event, performed with the company's usual table seating and food and drink service during the show.

events.name: Sidekicks Show: Improv Wars 3
events.description_short: Improv comedy from the Southside Sidekicks, the in-house troupe of Southside Theatre Guild.
events.description_full: Comedy made up on the spot: scenes and characters built live from audience suggestions, so no two shows are the same. Performed by the Southside Sidekicks, the in-house improv troupe of Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: Dictionary of the Vulgar Tongue
events.description_short: World-premiere satire: a pristine Connecticut living room, three centuries, and the question of who gets believed.
events.description_full: Alanna Coby's new play opens on affluent women gathered in a spotless Connecticut living room, certain of their own virtue — until a young working-class mother with nowhere else to go walks in. From there the play cracks time open across three centuries, tracing how class, gender, and power keep reproducing themselves under the cover of charity and good manners, and the comedy curdles into something darker. A finalist for the Alliance's own Kendeda Graduate Playwriting Competition and the Princess Grace Awards, it gets its world premiere from the Alliance Theatre, directed by Tinashe Kajese-Bolden. Runs through April 25.

events.name: A Streetcar Named Desire
events.description_short: Tennessee Williams' 1947 tragedy of Blanche DuBois, her illusions, and the brother-in-law who sees through them.
events.description_full: Tennessee Williams' 1947 drama, still one of the towering American plays. After losing the family home to creditors, Blanche DuBois arrives in a cramped New Orleans apartment to live with her sister Stella and a brother-in-law, Stanley Kowalski, who has no patience for her airs or her illusions. A flicker of hope appears in Stanley's courtly friend Mitch, but Blanche's past keeps circling closer. From Arcadia Theater, Alpharetta's community company.

events.name: Lauren Gunderson's The Book of Will
events.description_short: Gunderson's true story of the two actors who saved half of Shakespeare's plays by printing the First Folio.
events.description_full: After Shakespeare's death, his fellow actors Henry Condell and John Heminges set out to collect and publish his plays as the First Folio, without which roughly half of them would have vanished. Lauren Gunderson, the Atlanta-born writer who has repeatedly topped American Theatre's list of the country's most-produced living playwrights, spins the history into a story of friendship, grief, and stubborn devotion to the words. From the Atlanta Shakespeare Company, performed with the tavern's table seating and food and drink service.

events.name: Little Shop of Horrors
events.description_short: Menken and Ashman's doo-wop horror comedy: a skid row florist, a bloodthirsty plant, and a Faustian bargain.
events.description_full: The 1982 horror-comedy musical from Alan Menken and Howard Ashman, the songwriting team that later gave Disney The Little Mermaid. Meek flower-shop clerk Seymour discovers a strange plant that turns his fortunes around, but Audrey II has a taste for blood and an appetite that keeps growing. The score runs on early-'60s rock and roll, doo-wop, and Motown, with a girl-group trio of street urchins as Greek chorus. Presented by Stage Door Theatre.

events.name: The Clean House
events.description_short: Sarah Ruhl's whimsical comedy about a Brazilian housekeeper who'd rather craft the perfect joke than clean.
events.description_full: Sarah Ruhl's play, a 2005 Pulitzer finalist, starts with a mess: Lane, a doctor, has hired Matilde, a housekeeper who dreams of being a comedian and refuses to clean. Lane's sister Virginia, who finds cleaning a calling, secretly takes over the housework, and the discoveries in the laundry pull the whole household somewhere stranger and sadder. Variety praised its "screwy poetry and penetrating wisdom." Presented by Merely Players.

events.name: The Great American Trailer Park Musical
events.description_short: Nehls and Kelso's raunchy comedy of a stripper on the run and one very stuck marriage in a Florida trailer park.
events.description_full: David Nehls and Betsy Kelso's Off-Broadway comedy set in Armadillo Acres, a trailer park in Starke, Florida. Jeannie hasn't left her trailer since her son was abducted two decades ago; her tollbooth-collector husband Norbert hopes anniversary Ice Capades tickets will finally coax her out. Then Pippi, a stripper fleeing a marker-sniffing ex-boyfriend, moves in and complicates everything. From Pumphouse Players.

events.name: The Notebook
events.description_short: The Broadway musical of Nicholas Sparks's tearjerker romance, with songs by Ingrid Michaelson, on national tour.
events.description_full: Nicholas Sparks's 1996 novel — already one of the highest-grossing romantic film dramas ever adapted — reworked as a Broadway musical, with a score by singer-songwriter Ingrid Michaelson and a script by playwright Bekah Brunstetter. Allie and Noah come from different worlds and spend a lifetime finding their way back to each other; the musical splits each role among three actors playing them at different ages. A national touring production, following the show's 2024 Broadway run.

events.name: My Daughter, My Karma
events.description_short: Choreographer Hiroko Kelly blends contemporary dance and spoken word to trace inherited values across immigrant generations.
events.description_full: Choreographer Hiroko Kelly combines contemporary dance, spoken word, and original dialogue to explore intergenerational relationships and cross-cultural identity. Rooted in the second-generation immigrant experience, the piece examines how cultural memory, care, and inherited values move through the body across generations, asking how our ancestors' choices shape present-day karma. It considers the humanity and resilience of the immigrant experience, and the cultural practices that tether us to identity across time and place. Directed by Jacque Pritz.

events.name: The Last Five Years
events.description_short: Jason Robert Brown's two-person musical: her story told backward, his forward, crossing once on their wedding day.
events.description_full: Jason Robert Brown's song-cycle musical traces five years between Jamie, a novelist whose career takes off fast, and Cathy, an actress whose career won't. The trick is the structure: Cathy tells her story in reverse, starting at the marriage's end, while Jamie moves forward from the first date, and the two timelines meet only once, at the wedding. Brown wrote it after his own divorce, and the score ranges from klezmer to pop ballad. Presented by Junction Playhouse.

events.name: Something Rotten
events.description_short: Two Renaissance playwright brothers scheme to out-write Shakespeare by inventing the world's first musical.
events.description_full: The 2015 Broadway comedy by Karey and Wayne Kirkpatrick with John O'Farrell, set in 1595: brothers Nick and Nigel Bottom run a theatre troupe stuck in the shadow of a rock-star Shakespeare. Desperate for an edge, Nick pays a soothsayer to glimpse the future of theatre and learns it involves people bursting into song mid-scene. The score is wall-to-wall musical-theatre in-jokes; the show earned ten Tony nominations. From the Henry Players community theatre company.

events.name: Once Upon a Mattress
events.description_short: Mary Rodgers's 1959 musical spoof of The Princess and the Pea, from Players Guild at Sugar Hill.
events.description_full: The 1959 musical comedy that gave Carol Burnett her Broadway debut, with music by Mary Rodgers and lyrics by Marshall Barer, retelling The Princess and the Pea the way it supposedly really happened. Queen Aggravain has rigged an impassable test for any princess who wants to marry her son, and nobody else in the kingdom can wed until he does. Then Winnifred the Woebegone arrives, having swum the castle moat to get there. Presented by Players Guild at Sugar Hill.

events.name: Season 50 Gala
events.description_short: A benefit gala marking Theatrical Outfit's 50th season — the company has been at it since 1977.
events.description_full: A fundraising gala celebrating a milestone: Theatrical Outfit's 50th season. The downtown Atlanta company, founded in 1977, produces a mix of classic and contemporary plays alongside new-play development programs like Launchpad, and proceeds from the evening support that work.

events.name: The Welfare Queen
events.description_short: A two-person Southern drama: a food-stamp applicant and a rule-bound caseworker find how much they share.
events.description_full: A two-person play set inside a worn Department of Family and Children Services office in the contemporary South. Through humor, confrontation, and spoken word, it follows Idea Jasmine — a whimsical young woman applying for food stamps while pursuing her dream of opening a hair studio — and Masque Hollow, a blunt, rule-hugging caseworker nearing retirement. As their meetings unfold, both women confront the stories they've been told about poverty, motherhood, and success, until a public reckoning threatens the very office serving families like Idea's own. A new work by Atlanta playwright Brazil Remani.

events.name: Deadline
events.description_short: A mainstage play from Southside Theatre Guild, metro Atlanta's longest-running all-volunteer community theatre.
events.description_full: A mainstage production from Southside Theatre Guild, the all-volunteer community theatre that has been putting on shows since 1973 and bills itself as metro Atlanta's oldest continually running troupe of its kind. Everything here, from the acting to the set building, is done by volunteers.

events.name: The Mama Bear Repertory: The Winter's Tale and Mel Konner’s Sixteen Springs or Four Loves
events.description_short: Shakespeare's late romance in repertory with a new Atlanta play filling in its sixteen-year gap.
events.description_full: A repertory pairing from the Atlanta Shakespeare Company: Shakespeare's rarely staged late romance alongside a brand-new companion piece. In The Winter's Tale, a king's unfounded jealousy costs him his wife, his children, and his best friend before the story thaws into pastoral comedy and magic. On alternating dates, Sixteen Springs or Four Loves, by Atlanta playwright Mel Konner, dramatizes the sixteen years Shakespeare skips: the abandoned princess raised by shepherds, and Antigonus surviving his famous bear. The repertory runs through May 30.

events.name: Tosca
events.description_short: Puccini's melodrama of a diva, her painter lover, and Rome's predatory police chief, sung in Italian.
events.description_full: Puccini's 1900 melodrama of jealousy, torture, and betrayal, set in Rome in June 1800 with Napoleon's army on the march. The singer Floria Tosca and her painter lover Cavaradossi run afoul of Scarpia, the city's chief of police, who wants Cavaradossi dead and Tosca for himself. Musicologist Joseph Kerman famously dismissed it as a 'shabby little shocker,' yet it remains among the most performed operas anywhere, its score carrying 'Vissi d'arte' and 'E lucevan le stelle.' Presented by the Atlanta Opera.

events.name: Cat On A Hot Tin Roof
events.description_short: Tennessee Williams' Pulitzer-winning drama of Maggie the Cat, Brick, and a family gathering thick with lies.
events.description_full: Tennessee Williams' 1955 drama, winner of the Pulitzer Prize and his own personal favorite among his plays. On a Mississippi Delta plantation, the family of cotton tycoon Big Daddy gathers for his birthday, hiding what everyone but him knows about his health. At the center are Maggie the Cat and her husband Brick, a drinking ex-football hero who won't touch her, while his brother maneuvers for the family fortune. From Cherokee Theatre; recommended for ages 12 and up.

events.name: John Proctor is the Villain
events.description_short: Kimberly Belflower's Tony-nominated comedy-drama: Georgia teens study The Crucible and start doubting its hero.
events.description_full: An English class in a one-stoplight Georgia town takes up The Crucible, and the students begin to suspect Arthur Miller's hero isn't the good guy after all. Kimberly Belflower's play mixes pop music, first crushes, and feminist fury into what The New York Times called a "vital, hilarious, [and] thrilling" portrait of teenage girlhood. After a 2025 Broadway run that earned seven Tony nominations, including Best Play, it makes its Georgia debut from the Alliance Theatre, directed by Autumn Angelettie. Runs through June 6.

events.name: The Bodyguard
events.description_short: A musical theatre presented by Fox Theatre at Fox Theatre on Fri May 14, 7:30 PM ET.
events.description_full: Fox Theatre presents The Bodyguard, a musical theatre, at Fox Theatre on Fri May 14, 7:30 PM ET.

events.name: Arcadia
events.description_short: Tom Stoppard's 1993 comedy of chaos theory, Byron gossip, and one country house across two centuries.
events.description_full: Tom Stoppard's 1993 play unfolds in a single English country-house room across two centuries. In 1809, teenage math prodigy Thomasina Coverly scribbles ideas decades ahead of her time while her tutor dodges a duel over another man's wife. In the present day, two rival academics comb the same house for evidence of a scandal involving Lord Byron, and keep getting the past wrong. One 1993 critic grumbled it was 'too clever by about two-and-three-quarters'; many others rank it as Stoppard's finest play. From Pumphouse Players.

events.name: Sidekicks Time Machine - An Improvised Musical
events.description_short: A fully improvised musical from Southside Sidekicks — songs, scenes, and story invented on the spot.
events.description_full: A musical made up in front of you: the Southside Sidekicks, Southside Theatre Guild's in-house improv troupe, take audience suggestions and spin them into songs, characters, and a story that has never existed before and never will again. The time-machine premise gives the improvisers license to land anywhere in history the audience points them.

events.name: The Children
events.description_short: Lucy Kirkwood's darkly comic thriller: two retired nuclear physicists, a colleague back after 38 years, and old debts coming due.
events.description_full: In a cottage on the British coast, two married, retired nuclear physicists live quietly in the wake of a disaster. Then a former colleague reappears after 38 years, and the couple's careful equilibrium starts to crack. Lucy Kirkwood's 2016 thriller, funnier than its premise suggests, asks what one generation owes the one that follows; it was a hit in London and on Broadway, and in 2019 Guardian writers ranked it third among the greatest theatrical works since 2000. Presented by Theatrical Outfit.

events.name: Guess Who's Coming to Dinner
events.description_short: Todd Kreidler's stage adaptation of the 1967 film: a daughter's surprise fiancé tests her progressive parents' ideals.
events.description_full: Todd Kreidler adapts the 1967 film that starred Sidney Poitier, Spencer Tracy, and Katharine Hepburn, released the same year the Supreme Court struck down bans on interracial marriage in Loving v. Virginia. A liberal couple's daughter comes home unannounced with a new fiancé, and a celebratory dinner turns into a long night of the family arguing with its own principles about love, race, and acceptance. Funnier than its subject suggests, and pointed where it counts. Presented by Merely Players.

events.name: Roald Dahl's Matilda, the Musical
events.description_short: Dahl's telekinetic bookworm takes on the fearsome Miss Trunchbull, with songs by Tim Minchin.
events.description_full: Dennis Kelly and Tim Minchin's musical adaptation of Roald Dahl's novel about Matilda Wormwood, a brilliant little girl saddled with crooked parents and a child-hating headmistress who invents punishments for sport. Matilda fights back with wit, a fierce love of books, and a psychokinetic trick or two, finding an ally in her gentle teacher Miss Honey. First staged by the Royal Shakespeare Company, the show won a record seven Olivier Awards in 2012. This production comes from Arcadia Theater, the community company continuing three decades of theatre-making in its space.

events.name: The Hunchback of Notre Dame
events.description_short: Disney's stage musical of Hugo's bell-ringer tale, with the Menken and Schwartz score, from the Henry Players.
events.description_full: The stage musical drawn from Disney's 1996 animated film, itself adapted from Victor Hugo's 1831 Gothic novel. Quasimodo, the bell-ringer hidden away in Notre Dame's tower, longs for the world beyond the cathedral walls and finds a friend in Esmeralda, a Romani woman who stands up to his cruel guardian, Judge Frollo. The Alan Menken and Stephen Schwartz score is darker and more serious than most of the Disney songbook. A volunteer community production from the Henry Players.

events.name: Jubilee
events.description_short: An opera written and directed by Tazewell Thompson, librettist of Blue, with a score by Dianne Adams McDowell.
events.description_full: Tazewell Thompson wrote the libretto and directs; his previous opera, Blue, written with composer Jeanine Tesori, was named a New York Times Best in Classical Music pick for 2019. The score is by Dianne Adams McDowell, the Broadway vocal arranger behind A Gentleman's Guide to Love & Murder. The piece has already been staged at Arena Stage and Seattle Opera; this production comes from The Atlanta Opera.

events.name: Beetlejuice
events.description_short: The musical of Tim Burton's 1988 film: a grieving teen, two hapless ghosts, and a demon summoned by name.
events.description_full: The Broadway musical based on Tim Burton's 1988 film, with songs by Eddie Perfect and a book by Scott Brown and Anthony King. Lydia Deetz, a teenager mourning her mother and ignored by her father, moves into a house haunted by a recently deceased couple and a striped-suit demon who turns visible only if the living say his name three times. New York magazine's critic called it 'loud, cheeky, and all about excess,' true to its source. Expect 'Day-O' at the dinner party.

events.name: Stand Up for Improv - Improvised Stand-Up Show
events.description_short: Stand-up with no prepared material: comics improvise their sets live from audience suggestions.
events.description_full: A mash-up of two comedy forms: the performers take the stand-up mic, but every joke is invented on the spot, built from whatever the audience throws at them. No script, no tested material, no safety net. Presented by Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: Anthropology
events.description_short: A mainstage play from Pumphouse Players, one of Georgia's oldest continuously operating community theatre companies.
events.description_full: A play from Pumphouse Players, one of Georgia's oldest continuously operating community theatre companies. Their home is a converted 1940s cinema with just 70 seats, so no one sits far from the action.

events.name: Operation Mincemeat
events.description_short: SpitLip's Olivier-winning musical comedy about the real WWII scheme that fooled Hitler with a corpse and forged papers.
events.description_full: The true story from 1943: British intelligence dressed a dead body as a fictitious Royal Marines major, planted fake invasion plans in his briefcase, and floated him off the coast of Spain to draw Nazi troops away from Sicily. Ian Fleming, not yet the inventor of James Bond, is among the schemers. The musical comedy comes from SpitLip, the four-person British troupe who wrote it and originated the roles; it won the Olivier Award for Best New Musical in the West End, then transferred to Broadway, where Jak Malone won a Tony for his turn as the secretary Hester Leggatt.

events.name: Hairspray
events.description_short: The 2002 Tony-winning musical: a big-haired Baltimore teen dances her way onto TV and into the civil rights fight.
events.description_full: Baltimore, 1962: Tracy Turnblad, big of hair and bigger of heart, wins a spot on a local TV dance show and starts campaigning to integrate it. Marc Shaiman and Scott Wittman's score runs on 1960s dance-party pop and rhythm and blues, adapted from John Waters's 1988 film; the Broadway production took eight Tony Awards, including Best Musical. Presented by Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre running since 1973.

events.name: The Price is Ri-FUNNY
events.description_short: Live improv comedy — scenes and characters built on the spot from audience suggestions.
events.description_full: A night of improv comedy: nothing scripted, everything built in the moment from whatever the audience throws out, so no two performances are the same show. Presented by Southside Theatre Guild, an all-volunteer community theatre in operation since 1973 and home to the improv troupe Southside Sidekicks.

events.name: Every Brilliant Thing
events.description_short: Duncan Macmillan's one-performer play about a list of things worth living for, with the audience holding pieces of it.
events.description_full: Duncan Macmillan and Jonny Donahoe's one-performer play about a narrator who, at age seven, responds to his mother's suicide attempt by starting a list of everything worth living for. The list grows through college, marriage, and a depression of his own. The audience keeps the show running: some hold numbered cards and shout out entries on cue (rollercoasters, people falling over), and a few get drafted into roles on the spot. From Stage Door Theatre.

events.name: Death of a Salesman
events.description_short: Arthur Miller's 1949 Pulitzer-winning tragedy of Willy Loman, a salesman whose dreams keep outrunning his life.
events.description_full: Arthur Miller's 1949 tragedy, told through the memories, dreams, and arguments of Willy Loman, an aging traveling salesman who wants greatness for himself and his sons and cannot square that want with the life he actually has. It won both the Pulitzer Prize for Drama and the Tony for Best Play; the Daily Telegraph called it the most perceptive and moving play America had produced in a long time. The closing production of Theatrical Outfit's 50th anniversary season. Runs through September 30.
